An example of this would be a program or video game that, in addition to its offline function, also has online features that are set up and deactivated by the company accordingly.

they have no technical ownership of the product.

Moreover, due to the small or non-existent price difference and the unclear nature of the trade, most consumers cannot distinguish between licence and product.

paragraphs.text: On the basis of my personal experience in the unannounced removal of digital products purchased in the form of programmes, music, video games and additional or extended functions, I would like to ask the European Commission to grant consumers the right to permanent access to purchased digital goods. In my view, the current practice of companies to sell licences rather than to explicitly state this, but rather to ‘hidden’ in the terms and conditions of business or use, is limited to fraudulent trade. This is the equivalent of a car purchase in small print that you do not acquire the vehicle itself, but only the right to use it. This is compounded by the fact that the companies reserve the right to terminate these licences at any time, without prior notice or justification. In my same way, for example, one month after the alleged purchase of the vehicle, someone would recover the vehicle because the right of use was terminated. Consumers are currently sold licences for digital goods at the same or almost the same price as the physical copy of the corresponding goods, only that such licences have no specified or guaranteed duration and that access to the product is impossible without a licence. In theory, each of these licences could, in theory, expire or be terminated the next day, and the consumer could not object to it, since he or she agreed to the terms and conditions of use with the purchase. Moreover, due to the small or non-existent price difference and the unclear nature of the trade, most consumers cannot distinguish between licence and product. Furthermore, in the past, the firm has repeatedly deactivated some of digital products without compensating consumers for the depreciation of the product, as they have no technical ownership of the product. An example of this would be a program or video game that, in addition to its offline function, also has online features that are set up and deactivated by the company accordingly. In many cases, consumers do not even have the possibility to make an operational backup copy of the goods allegedly purchased offline, as they need an online verification to function. Once again, I would like to ask the European Commission to guarantee the consumer, even when buying one of these licences, the right to permanent access to a functional and complete product. I would like to thank you for your time and for your hearing.
